coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-copper nanocomposite-based fluorescent sensor for detection of creatinine in urine samples of CKD patients
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Bhatt, Poornima
Kukkar, Deepak
Yadav, Ashok Kumar
Kim, Ki-Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Creatinine (CR) is accepted as a clinical biomarker of chronic kidney disease (CKD) such as renal injury and kidney failure. To help facilitate the prognosis of CKD, a highly luminescent carbon dot (CD)-based fluorescent (FL) sensor has been built and employed for CR detection in diverse media (e.g., artificial and human urine). CDs, synthesized from sucrose precursor by a rapid microwave-assisted method (average diameter 20 nm), exhibited highly luminescent green emission upon UV exposure (λexcitation = 390 nm, λemission = 453 nm) with excellent temporal stability over three months. The nanocomposites are formed between CDs and metal ions (e.g., Cu2+) to realize the optimum biosensing of CR. Although Cu2+ ions showcases a maximum quenching (73 %) of the CDs, Cu2+/CDs system restores 77 % of the original FL intensity upon the addition of CR. The linear detection range and limit of detection for CR are estimated as 10-5 to 0.1 mg·dL-1 (R2 = 0.936) and 5.1 × 10-16 mg·dL-1, respectively. Furthermore, our biosensor shows excellent reproducibility and selectivity for CR in urine samples of healthy subjects and CKD patients. The Bland-Altman analysis for urine samples (n = 30) showcased an excellent agreement (R2 = 0.95) between our method and the gold standard ‘Jaffe’ method. These observations supported the practical utility of our method proposed for detection of CR in clinical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomarker
Biosensing
Chronic kidney disease
Fluorescence assay
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Korea Ministry of Science and ICT

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Research, Accreditation), Chandigarh University for providing the necessary research facilities. KHK acknowledges the financial support received from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) under the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304). PB performed the experimental tasks and wrote the original draft and worked upon the comments made by the supervisors. DK conceptualized the idea and supervised the research along with the initial editing and proofreading of the manuscript. AKY provided the clinical samples along with editing and proofreading of the manuscript. KHK co-supervised this research along with final editing and proofreading of the manuscript draft.

The authors are thankful to the Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Research, Accreditation), Chandigarh University for providing the necessary research facilities. KHK acknowledges the financial support received from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) under the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme depicting the synthesis of carbon dots as well as their images under daylight and UV light (365 nm).

Optical studies of carbon dots (CDs) (102 mg·dL-1 ) showing (A) UV–vis spectra (365 nm) and (B) FL emission spectra of CDs prepared using various concentrations of sucrose (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 g·mL−1) at λexcitation of 390 nm.

Characterization studies of carbon dots (CDs): (A) HR-TEM micrograph of CDs prepared from sucrose (0.6 g·mL−1), (B) EDS spectrum with peaks at 0–2 keV, (C) EDS image of CDs, (D) XRD pattern of CDs, (E) Zeta potential distribution of CDs. Scale bars in A and C corresponds to the size of CDs at 20 nm (HR-TEM) and 10 µm (EDS), respectively, and (F) FTIR spectrum of CDs prepared from sucrose (0.6 g·mL−1).

Effect of metal ions on the FL response of carbon dots (CDs): (A) The FL spectra (λexcitation = 390 nm) in the inset above are daytime and UV-light images of samples (from right to left CDs, Fe3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, and Cu2+) after addition of Mn+ on CDs and (B) the histogram showing the effect of various metal ions (0.1 mg·dL-1) on the fluorescence response of CDs (102 mg·dL-1).

The turn-on response in Cu2+ quenched carbon dots (CDs): (A) FL spectra show the FL turn-on response of CR toward Cu2+ quenched CDs and (B) Linear plot revealing FL enhancement of Cu2+ quenched CDs with increasing concentration of CR (10-5–10-1 mg·dL-1).
Sensitivity studies of Cu2+-quenched CD probe with various biomolecules (glucose (GLU), urea, and ascorbic acid (AA): 0.1 mg∙dL-1 each).

Real-world potential of Cu2+/CDs for CR recognition: (A) FL recovery of the nanocomposite upon addition of artificial urine sample comprising defined amounts of CR (9.8 mM) and (B) the specific response of CR to the Cu2+/CD nanocomposite coinciding with that of artificial urine with negligible variation in their FL intensity upon addition of various interfering biochemicals.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-copper nanocomposite-based fluorescent sensor for detection of creatinine in urine samples of CKD patients

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, Seongdong-gu, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, Seongdong-gu, Seoul 04763, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creatinine (CR) is accepted as a clinical biomarker of chronic kidney disease (CKD) such as renal injury and kidney failure. To help facilitate the prognosis of CKD, a highly luminescent carbon dot (CD)-based fluorescent (FL) sensor has been built and employed for CR detection in diverse media (e.g., artificial and human urine). CDs, synthesized from sucrose precursor by a rapid microwave-assisted method (average diameter 20 nm), exhibited highly luminescent green emission upon UV exposure (λexcitation = 390 nm, λemission = 453 nm) with excellent temporal stability over three months. The nanocomposites are formed between CDs and metal ions (e.g., Cu2+) to realize the optimum biosensing of CR. Although Cu2+ ions showcases a maximum quenching (73 %) of the CDs, Cu2+/CDs system restores 77 % of the original FL intensity upon the addition of CR. The linear detection range and limit of detection for CR are estimated as 10-5 to 0.1 mg·dL-1 (R2 = 0.936) and 5.1 × 10-16 mg·dL-1, respectively. Furthermore, our biosensor shows excellent reproducibility and selectivity for CR in urine samples of healthy subjects and CKD patients. The Bland-Altman analysis for urine samples (n = 30) showcased an excellent agreement (R2 = 0.95) between our method and the gold standard ‘Jaffe’ method. These observations supported the practical utility of our method proposed for detection of CR in clinical samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is generally degenerative with a poor prognosis [1]. It is particularly common in adults with diabetes and high blood pressure. Globally, around 10 % of adults with CKD have a poor quality of life [2]. As such, CKD accounts for half a million deaths and 28 million years of life lost (computed using the number of deaths attributed to CKD and the average life expectancy of people in different age groups) annually [3]. CKD is expected to rank as the fifth most common fatal disease worldwide by 2040 [4,5]. As CKD is associated with multiple negative healthconsequences, development of diagnostic protocols for its early detection would be beneficial.

Under clinical settings, serum creatinine (CR) is one of the most extensively analyzed biomarkers for a diagnosis of CKD [6,7]. CR is the by-product of creatine and creatine phosphate metabolism [8]. In healthy males and females, CR concentration ranges from 0.7 to 1.3 mg·dL-1 and 0.5 to 1.1 mg·dL-1, respectively [9]. In CKD patients, serum CR level may exceed the above values depending upon the extent of kidney failure. Therefore, precise determination of CR level can help manage the health of the individual through early diagnosis of CKD.

The conventional measurement of CR in clinical diagnostic laboratories has been based on the colorimetric Jaffe reaction [10,11]. This method exhibits good sensitivity and ease of automation to provide rapid detection of CR in clinical samples. Nevertheless, the sensitivity of the Jaffe method is affected by interfering metabolites (e.g., proteins, glucose, ketones, hemoglobin, bilirubin, pyruvic acid) and/or antibiotics (e.g., cephalosporine) [12–14]. Because of such limitations in the Jaffe method, researchers have been seeking alternative solutions for rapid detection of CR in clinical samples with appreciable sensitivity and selectivity. Among various sensing options, fluorescent (FL) sensing can offer excellent sensitivity and rapid detection of target analytes in complex sample matrices [15]. Numerous FL nanoprobes, such as metal nanoclusters (NCs), carbon dots (CDs), silica nanoparticles (NPs), semiconductor inorganic nanocrystals or quantum dots (QDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have been reported for the sensing of biomolecules [16,17]. Of these FL nanoprobes, CDs are sub-10 nm-diameter quasi-spherical NPs with numerous merits for biosensing applications such as inexpensive synthesis, biocompatibility, unique luminescent characteristics, tunable synthesis methods, numerous surface functionalities, and low toxicity [18,19]. These characteristics make CDs a prime choice for creating highly sensitive sensors for the detection of CKD biomarkers. Interestingly, CDs can be prepared using arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis, thermal combustion, hydrothermal, template, and microwave assisted methods [20–22]. Among these, the microwave process is a quick and affordable way to create CDs rapidly and cost-effectively with excellent product yield [23].

To date, there have been numerous reports on the FL-based detection of CR. For instance, glutathione-functionalized Cu NCs have been used for FL-based detection of CR in human serum [24]. The FL sensor showed a linear relationship in the CR range of 25 × 10-5–34 × 10-4 mg·dL-1 along with a limit of detection (LOD) value of 6.3 × 10-5 mg·dL-1. In another study, alkaline nitrogen-functionalized GQDs were used for FL detection of CR in human serum samples [25]. Recently, 2-mercaptopyrimidine (2-MPY) has been used to produce red-luminous Cu NCs [26]. The resulting 2-MPY-Cu NCs showed notable quantitative FL sensitivity to CR levels (6.25 × 10-6 –2 × 10-4) mg·dL-1), with an LOD of 9.91 × 10-7 mg·dL-1. However, the requirements of expensive precursors for metal NCs and the toxicity of QDs limit their clinical adaptation. Recently, nitrogen-doped CDs were reported for the FL ‘turn off–turn on’-based sensing of CR [27]. These CDs showed a linear detection range (LDR) and LOD value of 0–1.69 mg·dL-1 and 2.37 × 10-7 mg·dL-1, respectively. However, the nitrogen-doped CD surfaces did not respond uniformly to CR. These authors noted the highest (50 %) FL turn-on response for CR with only non-amino-rich nitrogen-doped CDs. Overall, the application of CDs for rapid and efficient FL detection of CR has been used scantily to date.

In view of these research gaps, in this study, the construction of a straightforward, inexpensive, and quick switch-off/switch-on FL sensor is proposed for sensitive and selective detection of CR. Specifically, CDs were synthesized through a microwave-assisted method using sucrose as a carbon source. The FL intensity of synthesized CDs was significantly quenched by Cu2+ ions, followed by effective restoration upon the addition of CR (Fig. 1 ). This ‘turn on’ FL analysis of CR was further extended to CR detection in spiked aqueous samples and artificial/human urine samples. Our biosensor showed excellent sensitivity in the detection of CR with the lowest limits. Our synthesized CDs exhibited outstanding stability (more than 3 months) and reproducibility for CR sensing across several aliquots. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the application of CDs has been used to detect CR in human urine samples (CKD patients).

Analytical grade chemicals (e.g., Fe(NO3)3 (99.95 %), Mn(NO3)2 (98 %), Ni(NO3)2 (98 %), Co(NO3)2 (99.9 %), Mg(NO3)2 (99.9 %), Zn(NO3)2 (98 %), Cu(NO3)2 (98 %), Hg(NO3)2 (98 %), Ba(NO3)2 (99 %), and Cr(NO3)3 (99 %)) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals, St. Louis, MO, USA. CR was obtained from Loba Chemie Ltd., Mumbai, India. Sucrose (C12H22O11) (99.9 %), ascorbic acid (AA) (98 %), acetic acid (CH3COOH) (99 %), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) (98 %), and urea (CH4N2O) (98.9 %) were purchased from Spectrum Chemicals, Mumbai, India. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity) provided by a Milli-Q EQ 7000 ultrapure water system. CaCl2 (97 %), MgSO4 (99.95 %), KH2PO4 (99.9 %), Na2SO4 (99.9 %), NaCl (99.5 %), and KCl (99 %) used in making artificial urine were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Ltd., Mumbai, India.

CDs were synthesized through an microwave irradiation method using sucrose as a carbon source [28,29]. For this purpose, different amounts of sucrose (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 g·mL−1) were added to separate glass bowls. Subsequently, 80 mL of ultrapure water was added to each glass bowl, followed by the addition of 20 mL of acetic acid (0.65 mol·L-1). In the next step, the reaction mixture was irradiated for 5 min in a microwave oven (power 800 W). After passive cooling (5–7 min), 20 g of NaHCO3 was added to each bowl to neutralize the acid first and to raise the pH to 10–12. The bowls were again irradiated for 5 min in the microwave oven to induce a suspension color change from light brown to dark brown within 10 min. This observation indicated the formation of aqueous stabilized CDs. The dark brown CD suspension was centrifuged at 12,298 g force (RCF) for 15 min and the pellet was oven dried (37 ± 2 °C). The CDs turned into light-brown flakes and were stored in the dark at 4 °C until further use. The CDs exhibited negligible variation in their FL emission intensity (λemission = 453 nm) when checked after 3 months of synthesis. These observations indicate the excellent stability of our NPs. Subsequently, the percentage (%) yield of CDs were estimated as follows: -
(1)
%
y i e l d
o f
C D s =
Amount
o f
C D s
o b t a i n e d
Amount
o f
r a w
m a t e r i a l
u s e d
× 100
In this study, sucrose of varying concentrations (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 g·mL−1) was used as raw material for synthesizing CDs.

For sensing CR, a nanocomposite of the CDs was prepared by adding various metal ions. Specifically, 0.1 mg.dL-1 stock solutions (10 mL) of each metal ion (e.g., Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, and Cr3+) were prepared in ultrapure water. Afterward, a 10-3 mg.dL-1 working solution of each metal ion was prepared by dilution of the respective stock solution. In the next step, 1 mL of CDs (102 mg·dL-1) was mixed with 2 mL of each metal ion in separate vials and incubated at 37 ± 2 °C for 10 min. The FL excitation and emission spectra of metal/CDs were recorded at specified wavelengths to check the variation in their FL intensities. Among the tested metal ions, Cu2+ ions exhibited the maximum quenching (73 %) of CDs.

In the next step, a 0.5 mg.dL-1 aqueous stock solution of CR was prepared by dissolving 0.05 mg of CR powder in 10 mL ultrapure water in a glass vial. The stock CR solution was diluted to yield various working CR concentrations (0.1, 0.05, 0.01, 0.005, 0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001, 0.00005, 0.00001 mg.dL-1) as shown in Table S1 (Supplementary Information (SI)). The prepared CR solutions (2 mL) were separately added to the Cu2+/CD (Cu2+= 10-3 mg.dL-1, 2 mL and CDs = 102 mg·dL-1, 1 mL) suspension prepared in the previous step and incubated at 37 ± 2 °C for 10 min. The FL spectra of the CR-treated Cu2+/CD suspension (total volume of each sample = 5 mL) were recorded using the spectrofluorometer at appropriate excitation (λexcitation = 390 nm) and emission (λemission = 453 nm) wavelengths.

The specificity of the Cu2+/CD-based FL sensor for CR was recorded in the presence of various interfering biochemicals (e.g., AA, glucose, and urea). For this purpose, 0.1 mg.dL-1 solutions (2 mL) of each chemical analyte were prepared in distilled water (DI) water. Subsequently, these solutions were incubated (individually and in a mixture comprising CR) with Cu2+/CDs for 10 min at 37 ± 2 °C. The FL spectra of these biochemically treated Cu2+/CDs was subsequently recorded relative to that of CR.

The practical utility of the Cu2+/CD-based FL sensor was further tested in artificial urine. As the first step, a stock artificial urine sample was prepared by following the procedures reported previously [30]. Specifically, 100 mL of DI water was mixed with the following quantities of each component to create artificial urine: CaCl2 (2.6 mM), MgSO4 (3.2 mM), KH2PO4 (19.8 mM), Na2SO4 (29.6 mM), NaCl (2.67 mM), KCl (1.55 mM), urea (310 mM), and CR (9.8 mM). The artificial urine sample (2 mL) was added to the Cu2+/CD suspension and their FL emission spectra were recorded as described above.

The performance of the sensor was also monitored in human urine samples (n = 30) at the Post Graduate Institute of Medical Education & Research, Chandigarh (PGIMER). The samples were stored at 4 °C and discarded after 24 h. Aliquots of the urine samples (100 µL) were treated similarly to calibration standards and their FL intensity (λexcitation = 390 nm) was monitored.

The analytical performance of the Cu2+/CD-based FL sensor was assessed through the LOD and limit of quantification (LOQ). These performance indicators were computed using the following formulae [31–33]:
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
K
(3)
LOQ =
10 σ
K
where σ is the standard deviation of the intercept, and K is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

The sensitivity of the Cu2+/CD-based FL sensor was also evaluated using the Stern-Volmer equation as follows [34,35]:
(4)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
(5)
K
SV
=
(
I 0
- I
I
)
×
1
Q
where I0 and I represent the FL intensity of the Cu2+/CDs in the absence and presence of CR, respectively; K SV is the quenching constant; and Q is the concentration of the CR.

A microwave instrument (LG 28 L Charcoal Convection Microwave Oven, South Korea) was used to prepare the CDs. The room temperature (RT) and FL measurements of all samples were studied using a spectrofluorometer (SHIMADZU RF-6000, Kyoto, Japan). The optical properties of samples were analyzed using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV–vis 2600 PC, Kyoto, Japan). UV–vis spectra were captured using quartz cuvettes with optical path lengths of 10 mm and a baseline correction across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The ultra-fine structure of CDs was analyzed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (JEOL JEM 2100 multipurpose, 200 kV analytical electron microscope, Tokyo, Japan) at Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. Further, the real-time energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of CDs was analyzed through a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JEOL JSM-IT500, Japan). The particle size distribution and zeta potential (ζ) of synthesized CDs were characterized using a particle size analyzer (Anton Paar Particle analyzer Litesizer 500, Vienna, Austria) in automatic mode at Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. The dynamic light scattering in the particle size analyzer was performed at the scattering angle of 90°, using a helium–neon (HeNe) laser light source, with a maximum intensity and wavelength of 10 mW and 632.8 nm, respectively. Additionally, an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs was measured through XRD D8 ADVANCE ECO (Bruker), Yokohama, Japan. The XRD spectrum was obtained by directing an X-ray beam (wavelength = 1.54 nm) at the sample and measuring the scattered intensity as a function of the outgoing direction. The intensity of diffracted X-rays was continuously recorded as the sample and detector rotated through their respective angles (5°–100°). The surface functional groups of synthesized CDs were identified using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS10, Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) at CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization (CSIO), Sector 30 C, Chandigarh, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs prepared by microwave irradiation were optimized by varying sucrose concentration (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 g·mL−1) as described above (Fig. 1). The percentage yield of CDs relative to sucrose concentrations were estimated as 30, 45, 66.6, 37.5, and 20 %, respectively. The prepared CD suspension exhibited green FL upon illumination under UV light (365 nm) as shown in Fig. 1. UV/Vis absorption spectrum of all CDs prepared using different sucrose concentrations showed an absorption peak at 368 nm (Fig. 2 A), which can be assigned to n → π* transition of C = O functionalities present in CDs [29]. The FL intensity of the CDs varied as a function of sucrose concentration (Fig. 2B). Specifically, CDs prepared from high (0.8 and 1 g·mL−1) and low sucrose levels (0.2 and 0.4 g·mL−1) showed low FL intensity relative to one prepared at intermediate sucrose level of 0.6 g·mL−1. CDs prepared from high sucrose levels (0.8 and 1 g·mL−1) were highly dense or concentrated. Therefore, excessively concentrated solution decreases the FL intensity as shown in Fig. 2B [36]. As such, CDs prepared from the sucrose (0.6 g·mL−1) were used for further CR sensing studies. The freshly prepared pristine CDs exhibited excitation (λexcitation) and emission (λemission) maxima at 390 and 453 nm, respectively. CDs prepared from 1 g·mL−1 sucrose concentration showed a red shift in λemission from 453 nm to 515 nm (Fig. 2B). This may reflect the increase in the extent of the π-electron system that would narrow the energy gap of the π-π* transitions [37,38]. This phenomenon lowers the energy levels to cause red-shifted FL emission of CDs. The FL spectra of CDs were also recorded after 3 months of synthesis to evaluate their colloidal stability. Accordingly, CDs exhibited negligible variation in FL intensity upon storage to confirm their outstanding colloidal stability (Fig. S1).

In the next step, HR-TEM analysis was performed to check the morphological characteristics of the CDs (Fig. 3 A). Accordingly, CDs were characterized by a uniform spherical shape and size distribution (standard deviation [σ] = 180.76) with an average diameter of ∼ 20 nm. The weight and atomic ratios of C and O of the carbon dots, as determined from SEM-EDS analysis, were 82.84:17.16 and 78.37:21.63, respectively (Fig. 3B and 3C). The crystallinity of the CDs was evaluated by the XRD measurement as shown in Fig. 3D. Three strong Bragg reflections at 2θ = 24.27°, 31.5°, and 42.8° in XRD spectrum were observed for the CDs. The peak at 24.27⁰ shows a slightly larger d-spacing than the 002 plane in graphite, which indicates the presence of a non-ideally arranged graphite-like structure [39]. The peaks at 31.5° and 42.8° were assigned to the 110 and 100 planes, respectively [40]. The XRD diffractogram of CDs was in complete agreement with the standard powder diffraction card of graphitic carbon (JCPDS26-1076) [41]. As such, the XRD studies confirmed the amorphous nature of CDs.

The ζ potential measurements revealed the surface charge of the synthesized CDs to be −19.3 mV (Fig. 3E). The high ζ potential attained possibly by the repulsion between negatively charged CDs indicates their excellent colloidal stability and dispersibility in pristine form. The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CDs demonstrated a characteristic broad peak at 3460 cm−1 attributed to the OH stretching vibration as seen in Fig. 3F [42]. The absorption peaks at 2425 and 1384 cm−1 was credited to C–H stretching vibrations and asymmetric stretching vibration of the COO– group, respectively [41]. The peaks at 1767 and 1639 cm−1 could be attributed to the presence of the C=O and C=C bands, respectively [43]. Finally, the FTIR absorption peak at 825 cm−1 demonstrated C–OH bending vibrations [44]. The presence of the aforementioned functional groups is suspected to contribute to the aqueous solubility of CDs.

For CR sensing, a nanocomposite was first prepared between CDs and metal cations (Mn+) (Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, and Cr3+). This step was performed to identify the Mn+ with maximum quenching of the CDs. The FL spectrum of all metal-treated CDs exhibited a substantial reduction in FL intensity as evident in Fig. 4 A. The addition of Mn+ to the CD suspension may help form coordinate bonds between these species [45]. As such, all the Mn+ are strongly attracted to the huge number of COO– and OH– groups on the surface of the CDs [29,46]. Upon absorption of Mn+, the inter-atomic distance between M-O increased and the angle between the plane of CDs and the metal atom was almost reduced to zero [47–49]. This should lead to the loss of radiative energy and subsequently culminates in the quenching of CDs.

In the next step, the variation in the FL quenching of Mn+-treated CDs was compared relative to that of pristine CDs. The relative quenching effect on Mn+-treated CDs was in the following order: Cu2+(72.90 %) ˃ Cr3+(39.52 %) ˃ Hg2+(40.10 %) ˃ Ba2+(37.91 %) ˃ Ni2+(36.16 %) ˃ CO2+(30.28 %) ˃ Zn2+(27.64 %) ˃ Mg2+ (27.28 %) ˃ Mn2+(25.57 %) ˃ Fe3+(24.06 %) (Fig. 4B). Among all the Mn+, our results explicitly demonstrate the highest quenching of CDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions. The high sensitivity and selectivity of CDs towards Cu2+ may be attributed to a large number of COO– and OH– groups on the surface of the CDs that have high binding affinity for Cu2+ [50]. These findings were consistent with those of previously reported studies [29,51]. Accordingly, the Cu2+/CD nanocomposite was chosen for the FL sensing of CR.

In the next step, the FL response of Cu2+/CDs toward CR was investigated. As expected, the addition of CR to the solution of CDs/Cu2+ led to restoration of their FL intensity (Fig. 5 A). In quantitative terms, the increase in FL intensity of CDs/Cu2+ demonstrated a linear increase as a function of CR concentration (10-5 mg.dL-1 to 10-1 mg.dL-1; R2 = 0.936) as shown in Fig. 5B. Additionally, our findings were similar across measurements performed in triplicate (relative standard error (RSE) = 4.2 %) to indicate the high reproducibility of our biosensor for CR detection (Fig. S2). These factors demonstrate the 'switch on' impact of CR on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. The LOD (calculated by the 3σ/ slope method) for FL sensing-based recognition of CR was determined to be 5.1 × 10-16 mg·dL-1. This value was 7–8 fold lower than that for the normal serum concentrations of CR as mentioned above. This low LOD result clearly demonstrates the remarkable sensitivity of our biosensor for CR identification in real samples, both qualitatively and quantitatively. The LDR for CR quantification was estimated to be 10-5 to 10-1 mg·dL-1. The sensitivity of Cu2+/CDs for CR recognition was also quantified in terms of K SV values at various CR concentrations (10-5 to 10-1 mg·dL-1) (Table S1). The K SV value for the Cu2+/CD-based FL sensor was calculated as − 1.7 × 10-10 mg−1·dL at a CR concentration of 10-5 mg·dL-1. The negative K SV value should correspond to the increase in the FL intensity relative to that of the pristine Cu2+/CD nanocomposite. Furthermore, the performance of our Cu2+/CD sensor was compared in terms of LOD, LDR, and K SV values for sensing different CKD biomarkers reported by some previous studies (Table S2). In terms of LOD, the top five biosensors were as follows: Cu2+/CDs (CR: 5.1 × 10-16 mg·dL-1) < CDs (N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase: 91.56 × 10-9 mg·dL-1) < 2-MPY-Cu NCs (CR: 99.1 × 10-8 mg·dL-1) < GSH-Cu NCs (CR: 0.63 mg·dL-1) < S,N-CDs (UA: 21.56 × 10-4 mg·dL-1). In terms of LDR, the five best performing biosensors were N-doped CDs (CR: 0–16.97 × 102 mg·dL-1) ˃ Fe, Co, N co-doped CDs (UA: 33.62 × 10-1 – 25.216 × 102 mg·dL-1) ˃ Cu2+/CDs (CR: 10-5–10-1 mg·dL-1) ˃ Si/N doped CDs (UA: 84.055 × 10-1–50.433 mg·dL-1) ˃ S,N co-doped CDs (UA: 13.448 × 10-1–16.811 mg·dL-1). Likewise, the sensitivity of various biosensors used for the detection of various CKD biomarkers was also compared in terms of Ksv values (Table S2). The biosensors with the lowest KSV values (in increasing order) were as follows: Cu2+/CDs (CR: −1.7 × 10-2 mg−1·dL) < N-SiNPs (CR: 1.56 × 10-6 mg−1·dL) < GSH-Cu NCs (CR: 11 × 10-2 mg−1·dL) < N-doped CDs (CR: 3.05 × 108 mg−1·dL) < RHB-Au3+ ion conjugate (CR: 2.94 × 1013 mg−1·dL). The lower LOD and KSV values for our Cu2+/CDs in comparison to studies reported previously indicate the excellent sensitivity of our sensor.

To explain the mechanism of Cu2+/CD-based CR detection, we propose an affinity-based ‘switch off’–switch on’ hypothesis. Notably, as illustrated in Fig. S3, CR is a lactam with an imidazole ring (two N-atoms with two lone pairs). The empty d-orbitals of Cu2+ atoms are drawn to the lone pair of the N-atoms of CR. This electrostatic attraction between Cu2+ atoms and CR may lead to reduced absorption of the Cu2+ ions over the surface of CDs. As a result, the FL intensity of CDs is recovered with increased addition of CR species to the Cu2+/CD suspension (Fig. S3).

To investigate the selectivity of CR, the effect of other comparable biomolecules (e.g., glucose, urea, and AA: 10-1 mg.dL-1 each) on the FL intensity of Cu2+/CDs was also investigated. These interfering biochemicals were chosen because of their coexistence with CR in the human body. None of the selected interferents caused an obvious increase in the FL intensity of the Cu2+/CD biosensor (Fig. 6 ). Among all the tested analytes, the highest FL recovery of Cu2+/CDs was observed in the descending order CR (76.89 %) > artificial urine (76.87 %) > glucose (35.08 %) > urea (35.06 %) > AA (34.61 %) (Fig. S4). Evidently, only CR produced a significant increase in the FL intensity of the Cu2+/CD nanocomposite to indicate excellent specificity.

To assess the practical utility of the Cu2+/CD biosensors, their performance was also validated against artificial urine. The addition of the artificial urine sample (2 mL) to the Cu2+/CD suspension restored the FL characteristics of the CDs as shown in Fig. 7 A and 7B. This observation demonstrated the excellent utility of our Cu2+/CD-based biosensor for quantitative determination of CR in complex samples. To validate the proposed method for the detection of CR, FL recovery analyses were also performed in human urine samples. The addition of 30 human urine samples having pre-defined CR concentrations (Table S3) to Cu2+/CD suspensions prepared in separate vials restored the FL characteristics of CDs. Moreover, the CR concentration computed using our method showed excellent agreement with those obtained with the clinically used ‘Jaffe’ method for similar samples (Table S3). For comparative analysis between the two measurement methods, Bland-Altmans and scatter plots were drawn as shown in Fig. S5. This analysis demonstrated a strong correlation (R2 = 0.95) between the two methods. These observations proved the real-life utility of our FL biosensor for detection of CR in human urine.

This research was carried out to demonstrate a novel CR detection approach based on a Cu2+/CD nanocomposite-based ‘switch off–switch on’ FL assay using green synthesized CDs. The synthesized CDs exhibited good aqueous solubility, excellent stability, and high FL intensity (λexcitation = 390 nm, λemission = 453 nm). Among various metal ions, the optimum quenching of FL intensity was attained by Cu2+ ions. The addition of CR led to a substantial recovery of the FL intensity of the Cu2+/CDs nanocomposite. The LDR, LOD, and Ksv values for CR recognition were estimated to be 10-5 to 10-1 mg·dL-1 (R2 = 0.936), 5.1 × 10-16 mg·dL-1, and −1.7 × 10-10 mg−1·dL, respectively. Moreover, the Cu2+/CD nanocomposite showed negligible restoration of FL intensity in the presence of comparable interfering biochemicals (e.g., glucose, urea, and AA). Our sensor showed high reproducibilityand selectivity for CR detection in aqueous media, artificialurine, and real samples. More specifically, our biosensor demonstrated excellent agreement with the clinically used “Jaffe” approach when used for CR detection in human urine samples. This finding demonstrated the applicability of our Cu2+/CD-based biosensor for quantifying CR in complex biological matrices. In the future, this research will be expanded to develop a quantitative FL lateral flow assay for CR monitoring in real urine samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Pro-Vice-Chancellor (Research, Accreditation), Chandigarh University for providing the necessary research facilities. KHK acknowledges the financial support received from the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) under the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) of the Korean government (Grant No: 2021R1A3B1068304).

ce:section.ce:para: PB performed the experimental tasks and wrote the original draft and worked upon the comments made by the supervisors. DK conceptualized the idea and supervised the research along with the initial editing and proofreading of the manuscript. AKY provided the clinical samples along with editing and proofreading of the manuscript. KHK co-supervised this research along with final editing and proofreading of the manuscript draft.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123666.
